coredata.dc:title: Application of electrochemical sensors based on nanomaterials modifiers in the determination of antipsychotics
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Jiao
Zhang, Lijing
Liu, Zhiling
Ling, Guixia
Zhang, Peng
coredata.dc:description: At present, the content of antipsychotics in samples is always analyzed by traditional detection methods, including mass spectrometry (MS), spectrophotometry, fluorescence, capillary electrophoresis (CE). However, conventional methods are cumbersome and complex, require a large sample volume, many pre-processing steps, long analysis cycles, expensive instruments, and need well-trained detection capabilities personnel. In addition, patients with schizophrenia require frequent and painful blood collection procedures, which adds additional treatment costs and time burdens. In view of these factors, electrochemical methods have become the most promising candidate technology for timely analysis due to their low cost, simple operation, excellent sensitivity and specificity. As we all know, nanomaterials play an extremely important role in electrochemical sensing applications. As the sensor modifiers, nanomaterials enable electrochemical analysis to overcome the time-consuming and labor-intensive shortcomings of traditional detection methods, and greatly reduce the research cost. Nanomaterials modified electrodes can be used as sensors to determine the concentration of antipsychotics in organisms quickly and accurately, which is a bright spot in the application of nanomaterials. The combination of different nanomaterials can even form a nanocomposite with a synergistic effect. This paper firstly reviews the application of nanomaterials-modified sensors on the basis of research in the past ten years, reviews the use of nanomaterial-modified sensors to quickly and accurately determine the concentration of antipsychotics in biological samples, and demonstrates a new idea of using nanomaterials sensors for drug monitoring and determination. At the end of this review, a brief overview is given of the limitations and the future prospects of nanomaterial sensors for the determination of antipsychotics concentrations.

Sensors
Antipsychotics

row.entry: CAGs and metal compound NPs composite

head.ce:title: Application of electrochemical sensors based on nanomaterials modifiers in the determination of antipsychotics

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, No. 103, Wenhua Road, Shenyang 110016, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, No. 103, Wenhua Road, Shenyang 110016, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: At present, the content of antipsychotics in samples is always analyzed by traditional detection methods, including mass spectrometry (MS), spectrophotometry, fluorescence, capillary electrophoresis (CE). However, conventional methods are cumbersome and complex, require a large sample volume, many pre-processing steps, long analysis cycles, expensive instruments, and need well-trained detection capabilities personnel. In addition, patients with schizophrenia require frequent and painful blood collection procedures, which adds additional treatment costs and time burdens. In view of these factors, electrochemical methods have become the most promising candidate technology for timely analysis due to their low cost, simple operation, excellent sensitivity and specificity. As we all know, nanomaterials play an extremely important role in electrochemical sensing applications. As the sensor modifiers, nanomaterials enable electrochemical analysis to overcome the time-consuming and labor-intensive shortcomings of traditional detection methods, and greatly reduce the research cost. Nanomaterials modified electrodes can be used as sensors to determine the concentration of antipsychotics in organisms quickly and accurately, which is a bright spot in the application of nanomaterials. The combination of different nanomaterials can even form a nanocomposite with a synergistic effect. This paper firstly reviews the application of nanomaterials-modified sensors on the basis of research in the past ten years, reviews the use of nanomaterial-modified sensors to quickly and accurately determine the concentration of antipsychotics in biological samples, and demonstrates a new idea of using nanomaterials sensors for drug monitoring and determination. At the end of this review, a brief overview is given of the limitations and the future prospects of nanomaterial sensors for the determination of antipsychotics concentrations.

ce:section.ce:para: Psychosis is a mental illness that is clinically manifested as cognitive diseases, such as schizophrenia and delusions [1], and as a serious problem affecting millions of people around the world and causing a huge economic and medical burden [2,3]. Nowadays, antipsychotics provide the possibility for the treatment of psychosis, and the concentration of which in the body is extremely important for whether to play a therapeutic effect. If excessive, it will cause many side effects and cause harm to the health of people. Therefore, due to the wide application of antipsychotics, rapid, economical and practical methods are required urgently.

Electrochemical sensors have important impacts on people's daily lives，especially for clinical applications. Compared with traditional detection methods, including mass spectrometry (MS) [4], spectrophotometry [5], fluorescence [6], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [7], and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [8], electrochemical analytical methods are most promising, which offer several practical benefits, including affordable and small-scale instruments, real-time analysis, and more selective to interference compared with the traditional ones [9–12]. However, the traditional methods are often time-consuming and costly.

As non-invasive diagnostic tools, nanomaterials can combine multiple modes in a single sample well and have versatility and potential, which are conducive to improving detection sensitivity [13]. Therefore, modifying the electrode surface with nanomaterials can be a novel route to develop high-sensitivity sensors so that the electrochemical response can be improve significantly [14–18]. Additionally, in order to further improve the electrocatalysis, two or more modified nanomaterials can also be used to manufacture sensors [19–21]. So far, indicators, inorganic complexes [22], surfactants [23–25], etc, are usually used as modifiers to modify electrodes. Among many modifiers, nanomaterials are the most shining existence, which include different nanoparticles (NPs) [26], nanowires, nanorods, nanoflowers [18,19], etc, which are superior candidates for electrochemical sensing. In this paper, the applications of kinds of nanomaterials as modifies in antipsychotics sensing are reviewed firstly.

Nanomaterials play an important role in many fields because of their excellent properties. From the past few decades to the present, many reviews and overviews of nanomaterials had been produced. However, to the best of our knowledge, there are not many articles on the application of nanomaterials as electrode modifiers for the determination of antipsychotics concentrations. However, it is undeniable that nanomaterials have considerable development prospects in the field of biomedical sensing assays due to their unique thermoelectric properties. This paper reviews the types and applications of nanomaterials used as electrode modifiers to determine the concentration of antipsychotics. By searching for articles in the “Web of Science” with the keywords as nano* , sensor, and antipsychotic, the screening years are from 2010 to 2022, and the total number of articles is 66. Since 2018, the number of publications on this topic has begun to increase, which means the topic is relatively novel and has great research prospects. Therefore, this paper is of great significant for this field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and sensing mechanism of nanomaterial sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The general procedure to fabricate the nanomaterial sensors can be divided into 3 steps. Firstly, polish the bare electrode with alumina slurry, and then, rinse with distilled water, next, sonicate it in a mixture of distilled water and methanol (1:1) for several minutes, and then, rinse the electrode again with distilled water to remove impurities. Ultimately, add the nanomaterial modifier dropwise to cover the dry and clean electrode, and dry it at 50 °C for 30 min

At present, there are about 100 kinds of antipsychotic drugs in clinical use. From the view of molecular structure, the most commonly used ones are phenothiazines, tricyclics, diphenoxazapines, benzodiazepines, and 5-hydroxytryptamine re-intake inhibitors. Most antipsychotics are polycyclic or heterocyclic macromolecular organic compounds with high molecular weight, difficult electron transfer, and low electrochemical activity. However, with the rapid development of current material preparation technology, a large number of new electrochemical sensors for antipsychotics continue to emerge. Modified sensors have been widely applied in the electrochemical determination of antipsychotics, and a series of research results have been obtained. On the one hand, modification and tailoring of the electrode improves the affinity/hydrophobic environment of the interface and enhances the effect of drug enrichment. On the other hand, by means of self-assembly, coating, deposition, polymerization, etc., functional nanomaterials are constructed on the surface of the electrode, through their unique surface effect, catalytic activity, size effect and quantum effect, the conductivity of the electrode is enhanced, the transfer of electrons at the interface is accelerated, and the electrochemical behavior is promoted. These two points can be effectively combined to exert a synergistic effect, and the electrochemical response of the drug molecule will be significantly enhanced, thereby greatly improving the detection sensitivity. Antipsychotics can transfer electrons on the surface of the electrodes, which are converted into electrical signals to be monitored.

Metal NPs have excellent characteristics such as high surface area, low detection limit (LOD) and easy surface functionalization, and are widely used as sensor modifiers [27]. Metal NPs include noble metal NPs, general transition metal NPs and rare metal NPs. Noble metal NPs, such as Pt, Au and Ag, are particularly interesting due to their unique shape-related optoelectronic properties [28,29]. Noble metal nanomaterials possess fascinating size-dependent electrical, optical, magnetic, and chemical properties have received extensive attention for their many technological applications. Noble metal NPs are one of the most widely studied nanomaterials due to their ease of synthesis, characterization, and surface functionalization.

For example, under the condition of pH 7.0, bare CPE, lycopene (Saf)/CPE, Ru/CPE and Ru-Saf/CPE sensors are used for electrochemical detection of amisulpride (AMS) and the corresponding voltammogram is shown in Fig. 1A [30]. The obtained anodic peak current (Ip) of bare CPE is 35.0 μA, however, the Ip of Saf/CPE sensor elevated to 39.0 μA, and the Ip of Ru/CPE further increases at 52.0 μA, which is attributed to the high conductivity and larger surface of Ru NPs. Therefore, metals and its oxide NPs can be used as promoters of the electron transfer process and electrodes. Fig. 1B shows that the redox current of the CPE is relatively weak, but when the CPE is modified with Ru-Saf, the redox current is significantly increased to 2.6 times.

Transition metal sulfide (TMS) is the main mineral in nature with unique properties and different structure types providing a rich application [31]. In addition, nanostructured TMS has been effectively applied to various applications such as lithium-ion batteries, solar cells, thermoelectric devices and sensors due to its ability to explain quantum size effects [32,33]. Sangili et al. [34] prepared a bimetallic sulfide refined sensor to detect trifluoperazine (TFP). The sensor has excellent electrochemical properties, low LOD and high sensitivity. Sakthivel et al. [35] synthesized nanospheres consisting of WS2-RuS2 to modified GCE and to quantify pimozide (PMZ) with a low LOD as 0.2 nM. Transition metal ferrite is a new class of nanomaterials, attracting amount concentration, because of its excellent properties. Copper ferrite NPs (CuFe2O4) are one of them, which are excellent materials for catalysis [36–38], photocatalysis [39], dye detection [40], and solar cell anode materials [41].

A nano-composite modified CPE was constructed using copper ferrite modified carbon nanofibers (CNF-CuFe2O4), a voltammetric sensor for measuring PMZ with high sensitivity, selectivity ( Fig. 2). The CuFe2O4 NPs are spherical, and the porosity remains intact after CNF doping, indicating that the nanocomposite has high structural stability. Compared with bare CPEs, CNF-CuFe2O4 nanocomposite modified CPEs oxidize PMZ at a lower potential and high Ip value. Compared with bare electrodes, CNF and CuFe2O4 NPs have higher conductivity and larger electrochemical specific surface area. When used in actual sample analysis, the CNF-CuFe2O4/CPE sensor has a recovery rate of 99% for PMZ. Therefore, Transition metal ferrite has great application potential in clinical electrochemical analysis [42].

However, several major drawbacks limiting the application of metal NPs in sensors are their thermodynamic instability and tendency to aggregate, resulting in poor sensor stability and low sensitivity due to their high surface free energy.

Carbon-based nanomaterials, such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene, carbon-based quantum dots (CQDs) and various other types of nanomaterials, have received great attention due to their promising sensor applications, and they are most widely used in various compounds electrochemical sensing [43,44]. Carbon-based nanomaterials offer several advantages due to their unique properties, such as high specific surface area, high electrical conductivity, chemical stability, biocompatibility, and strong mechanical strength.

Graphene has unique physical and chemical properties, including excellent mechanical characters, conductivity, stability and biocompatibility [45]. Graphene nanosheets can be simply processed-easily exfoliated from earth-abundant graphite, which are thought to be allotropic to fullerenes and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [46]. Graphene and its oxygen-containing derivatives, including graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (GR), are emerging as an important class of nanomaterials in the field of biosensors. GO is the most significant derivative type of graphene. GO has a hydrophilic group which is conducive to chemical functionalization. Other nanomaterials can be modified by interacting with groups, which is benefit for dispersion and provides sites to enable active nanomaterials to bond with graphene sheets [47,48]. The larger surface area makes them conducive to electrocatalytic reactions and sensing capabilities [49,50]. By integrating nanomaterials on the surface of graphene, the aggregation puzzle is easy to reduce or prohibit [51]. Graphene and its derivatives are used in electronics, sensing, and biomedicine, which have been extensively researched [52–54]. In particular, hexagonal boron nitride (HBN), also known as white graphene, has the same excellent physical and chemical properties as graphene has, and has received wide attention in sensing and biological transmission. Graphene-based sensors, which exhibit less electrical noise and crystal defects than conventional sensing materials, play a critical role in electrochemical sensor applications. Electrochemical sensors developed using graphene can be operated using simpler techniques than traditional methods such as MS.

Although a great deal of research work has been done on sensors based on graphene and its derivatives, there are still some challenges that need to be addressed. The manufacture of graphene is a complex and expensive process, which takes a lot of time to generate high-quality graphene and consumes a large amount of raw materials. The technology to generate low-cost graphene has not yet been commercialized. Some catalysts used in graphene growth can increase its toxicity, which makes it difficult to use for biomolecular detection and other biomedical applications [55]. The susceptibility of graphene to be oxidized in an oxidizing environment also makes it extremely difficult to utilize graphene in its pure form. When using GO to produce GR, although the electrochemical activity can be improved, the chemical reduction leads to a huge mass loss, resulting in a very small amount of sample generated [56]. The use of graphene-based nanocomposites to modify electrodes may affect the intrinsic properties of the materials, but the electrical conductivity and thermal stability of the composites are also reduced compared to graphene alone. Although it is possible to solve the above problems with additional steps such as chemical or thermal reduction, it may become complicated and increase the cost [57,58].

QDs are fluorescent semiconductor NPs with favorable chemical properties, unique optical properties, high quantum yield and adjustable size, which can improve the sensitivity of biological imaging at the cellular level [59]. CQDs and graphite QDs (GQDs) are novel types of carbon-based nanomaterials, which are widely used as biosensors due to their excellent optical and biocompatibility, excellent electronic properties, strong chemical inertness, large specific surface area, abundant functionalized edge sites [60,61].

CQDs are defined as NPs, mainly composed of carbon, which can be easily produced from various raw materials and possess strong chemical inertness. Most semiconductor QDs contain heavy metals, leading to adverse environmental and biohazardous effects, hindering their biomedical applications [62–64]. CQDs have been proposed as promising alternatives to conventional QDs, because they do not require cumbersome and expensive preparation steps, do not contain toxic metal ions and have high biocompatibility. However, unlike other carbon-based nanomaterials, CQDs have fewer applications [65].

GQDs are zero-dimensional (0D) nanostructures from the carbon family. Unlike CQDs, GQDs have a graphene structure inside the dots and are considered as good electron transporters. Despite the excellent characteristics of both types of QDs, GQDs have far more applications in electrochemical biosensing than CQDs. GQDs are ideal candidate materials for sensors modification due to their oxygen-containing structure, favorable biocompatibility and large surface area, and may replace graphene, GO, or GR in the future. GQDs can overcome some of the disadvantages associated with the non-conductive properties of graphene oxide, as well as the lack of functional groups for biomolecule attachment in pure graphene [66–68]. However, a wide variety of commercial GQDs are not yet available, and their synthesis is laborious and lacks reproducibility. In general, the synthesis of QDs with precise dimensions is not easily reproducible, and there is a possibility of agglomeration and formation of large-sized QDs [69]. In addition, the conductivity of GQDs is very poor, to improve this performance, doping with heteroatoms is an ideal method, such as N, S, P and other substances are doped in the core of GQDs to increase adsorption sites and improve chemical activity [70].

CNTs are tubular structures with a diameter of 1–2 nm [71]. They can be metals or semiconductors and are similar to self-rolling graphite plates in structure. They are single-walled carbon CNTs (SWNTs), double-walled CNTs (DWNTs) or multi-walled CNTs (MWNTs), respectively. CNTs have low toxicity and non-immunogenicity [72]. Functionalized CNTs, which have excellent optical and thermal properties, can traverse biological barriers through transcellular and passive mechanism [73]. Recent studies have shown that adding metal NPs will greatly improve the electrocatalytic activity of nanocomposites. The combination of metal NPs and CNTs can significantly increase the conductivity of the electrode, and facilitate the construction of sensors with improved analytical performance. However, compared with the most significant facile modification advantages offered by graphene and QDs in electrochemical sensor development, widely used CNTs are not outstanding. But in fact, the structural similarity of GQDs and CNTs enables the interaction between the two nanomaterials through strong π-π interactions, resulting in a hybrid nanomaterial that incorporates the fascinating properties of the individual components.

CAGs are special solid-state materials that combine the structural and physicochemical properties of nanoscale building blocks with the unique properties of aerogels, such as large surface area, high porosity, and low density. Therefore, CAGs have fast response signals, high selectivity, and ultra-sensitivity, which can be used for sensing different targets. Recently, carbon-based aerogels (CAGs) with a specific internal structure have attracted widespread attention in the electrochemical researches, such as sensors and energy storage device [74]. In addition, the modification of CAGs with metal elements or other nanomaterials will improve carbon behavior. For example, the bimetallic NPs decorated on CAG are manufactured with extraordinary electrochemical and electroanalytical performance. Veerakumar et al. [75] prepared a CAG sensor modified with ultrafine Bi-Sn NPs (Bi-Sn NPs/CAG) to determine the content of CLZ, and the LOD was 31.3 nM. The selectivity of CLZ was improved when the concentration of potential interfering active substance was 10 times. This sensing method could quickly determine the amount of CLZ in rat brain serum samples ( Fig. 3). The development of high-performance CAGs sensors has yielded many promising results, but in-depth research is still lacking. The problem of large-scale and low-cost production of high-quality aerogels has not been adequately addressed. Maintaining the porous structure of aerogels, especially the microporous-structure during sensor design, is also an important issue. During the preparation process, the microporous-structure of CAGs may be damaged, and the introduction of thin aerogel films as sensing substrates can prevent this damage to a certain extent.

Nanodiamonds (NDs), a new type of carbon material, have a wide range of applications and a moderate price. NDs with a high surface area, are easy to surface functionalization. In addition, NDs have certain redox behavior, biocompatibility, non-toxicity and chemical inertness, which are suitable for in vivo analysis [76]. However, NDs are artificially synthesized by the detonation of explosives, which is dangerous.

Cellulose is abundant in nature, and nanocellulose (NCE) in particular is considered as a reliable green substrate for bio-nanocomposites for various applications. Due to its high specific surface area and tunable surface chemistry, NCE exhibits excellent mechanical reinforcement properties comparable to conventionally used materials. Furthermore, NCE is a cost-effective, biodegradable, environmentally friendly and biocompatible nanomaterial [77], which can improve the stability and sensitivity of electrochemical sensors. In addition, NCE contains abundant hydrophilic groups, which can be further modified to selectively bind analytes. This feature makes it an ideal coupling material for other nanomaterials such as graphene [78] and MWNTs [79]. However, to fully realize nanocellulose nanocomposites as realistic multi-sensing platforms in practical terms, some key challenges need to be addressed and considered. The primary challenges are the standardization of the synthesis of NCE sources and the characterization of nanoscale size structures, surface functional groups, and assembly behavior. The applications of these two carbon-based nanomaterials for determination of antipsychotics will be introduced in the Section 4.4.3.

In addition to metal and carbon-based nanomaterials, there are also some nanomaterials used to modify electrodes, such as ionic liquids (ILs) and molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs). ILs have already drawn extensive attention in the electrochemical area because of their high ionic conductivity, oxidation, thermal stability and non-flammability [80,81].

MIPs are highly selective and sensitive to target molecules [82,83]. The recognition capability of MIPs is provided by the placement of template molecules in the polymer network during synthesis. In addition, MIPs have many advantages such as long-term stability, easy and low cost of preparation, potential reusability, microbial resistance (compared to biometric systems), and excellent adaptability to various transducers ability. The sensor manufactured by the electrode with MIPs modified has simple preparation process and fast response time. In addition, they always form nanocomposites with other nanomaterials to modify the electrodes together and to achieve a multiplier effect with half the effort. Electrodes based on nanocomposites materials with synergistic effects are expected to increase the sensing ability of the electrodes several times by reducing the overpotential and increasing the sensitivity. Various nanomaterial modifiers involved in the fabrication of antipsychotics sensors. However, MIPs have been plagued by gradual template elution, which is due to the nature of the highly cross-linked polymer texture, the templates absorbed during the preparation stage are difficult to remove completely. Incomplete removal of templates from textures can lead to significant problems in evaluating rebinding assays, separation efficiency, etc.

ILs are a class of organic salts that exist as liquids below 100 °C. ILs have excellent ionic mobility, thermal stability, catalytic properties, and biocompatibility. In addition, ILs have excellent properties such as high electrical conductivity, wide electrochemical window, high stability, low volatility, moderate viscosity, non-flammability, and low melting point. The excellent ionic conductivity and wide electrochemical window open up new avenues for the construction of electrochemical devices. The high biocompatibility of ILs facilitates the loading of biomolecules on the electrode surface and the ability to achieve ultra-low LOD. However, the complexity of most chemical reactions makes it impossible to achieve the purity required for electrochemical studies of ILs using a single method, and a combination of techniques may be required to improve purification efficiency [84].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of antipsychotics by NPs-based sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The usage of nanomaterials as modifiers to modify electrodes is the key to the determination of biological samples by sensors [85–87]. Due to the excellent properties of nanomaterials, sensing can be carried out faster and easier, which will be promoted by the joint of a variety of nanomaterials. This chapter mainly introduces the application of nanomaterials modified sensors in the determination of antipsychotics. The co-modification of a variety of nanomaterials can be between carbon-based materials, or metal materials and carbon-based materials. A variety of nanomaterials form nanocomposites, which are modified on the electrode, thereby greatly enhancing the sensing ability.
Carbon-based nanomaterials can enhance the electrochemical performance of sensors, and have been used as conductive carriers in electrochemical sensors, e.g., G, GR and CNTs.

MWCNTs have excellent inductance and electrical conductivity, significant mechanical strength and catalytic performance [88]. Khalil et al. [89] assembled a modified CPE for potential analysis of sulpiride (SLP). The sensor combined MWCNTs showed sufficient selectivity for SLP, with a LOD of 3.5 × 10−7 mol·L−1, which could successfully determine SLP with high accuracy. Lawrywianiec [90] built a quetiapine (QTF) sensor based on CB NPs, under the optimal conditions, the LOD was 7 × 10−9 mol·L−1. This method has been maturely used for monitoring of QTF in drugs and human urine, and the recovery is between 99% and 107%. Stankovic et al. [91] described a simple and efficient electrochemical determination of TFP with a LOD of 0.7 μmol∙L−1 under optimized conditions. The proposed method was applied analytically by determining TFP content in model human urine samples with good accuracy. The developed method facilitates the analysis of TFP in biological samples using NDs electrodes as electrochemical sensors.

Metal nanomaterials have good electrochemical properties. Kaleeswarran et al. [92] prepared In3+ doped CuBi2O4 NPs to determine chlorpromazine selectively and sensitively (CPZ). The modified electrode has good catalytic performance, wide response range and low LOD (1.2 nM). The picture showed that the IP current increases significantly with increasing concentration, which verified the sensor was feasible as a CPZ quantitative electrode material.

Metal tungstate, especially tungstic acid (Yb2WO6), has excellent electrochemical stability and good electrical conductivity. Sundaresan et al. [93] synthesized scheelite Yb2WO6 NPs. Compared with the unmodified electrode, the electrochemical behavior of Yb2WO6 NPs modified screen-printed carbon electrode (Yb2WO6/SPCE) has been significantly improved. The electrode showed high selectivity, sensitivity and low LOD for CPZ. Mashhadizadeh et al. [94] developed a TiO2 NPs-carbon paste electrode (CPE) chemically modified by TiO2 NPs to determine CLZ, and the LOD was 61.0 nM. Chavada et al. [95] used rhodamine B-modified silver NPs (Ag NPs) to determine olanzapine (OLZ) based on the dual-reading assay. This method showed excellent selectivity to OLZ in the presence of multiple antipsychotics, cations, sugars and amino acids. Ghanbari et al. [96] used titanium dioxide NPs and copper oxide (TiO2 NPs@CuO) modified CPE to determine CLZ, and the LOD was 9 pM. Asran et al. [30] developed a Ru-Saf/CPE sensor using Ru NPs and Saf as modifiers. The sensor can simultaneously determine AMS in the presence of citalopram (CIT), which demonstrated electrocatalytic activity and selectivity and exhibited a low LOD of 8.80 × 10−9 M. Kesavan et al. [97] synthesized FeV NPs/30 HBN nanocomposite to modify SPCE, and the LOD was 0.004 μM. Fig. 4A shows the CV response of different sensor, and Fig. 4B shows the FeV NPs/30HBN electrode has a higher oxidation current. Therefore, FeV NPs/30HBN nanocomposite was selected as the sensitive electrochemical detection material of CPZ (Fig. 4). Then, they used FeV NPs for the detection of thioridazine hydrochloride (TDZ). The FeV NPs-modified SPCE electrode exhibited excellent catalytic activity for the electro-oxidation of TDZ with a LOD of 0.008 µM and excellent selectivity in the presence of organic and inorganic compounds [98]. Vinothkumar et al. [99] developed an electrochemical sensor based on Ni@CeO NPs for high-sensitivity detection of TFP. Under the optimized voltammetry technique, the modified SPCE showed a low LOD of 0.007 µM, which would be effectively used to detect TFP in human urine and serum samples with good recovery.

MIP NPs represent a method of creating synthetic materials that have selective recognition properties for the desired template, and are stable polymer materials with high selectivity. A novel electrochemical sensor modified by MIP NPs by Motaharian et al. [100] was used to determine QTP. Under the optimum conditions, the LOD was counted to be 5.04 × 10−9 M. Then, using MIP NPs as recognition elements, Panahi et al. [101] developed a novel electrochemical sensor for selective and sensitive determination of the benzodiazepine antipsychotics midazolam (MDZ). Compared with other benzodiazepines, including oxazepam, diazepam, and alprazolam, the proposed sensor exhibits excellent selectivity for MDZ. MIP NPs were synthesized by precipitation polymerization and then added to the composition of CPE as sensing elements. The MDZ assay achieved high sensitivity and low LOD as 1.77 × 10−10 M. The sensor was successfully used to analyze MDZ in pharmaceutical preparations and human urine samples. The results demonstrated that the MIP NPs modified CPE would be a promising sensor for the selective detection of MDZ at trace levels in real samples with complex matrices.

By using one or more nanomaterials to prepare the sensors, not only the sensitivity can be improved, but also the antifouling effect, repeatability, lasting stability and electrochemical reaction are also enhanced [102]. CNTs and GQDs are the two most important carbonaceous materials. They are often coupled with metal and its oxide nanomaterials to form nanocomposites [103]. The electrode sensing capability based on this material will be increased several times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon-based and metal compounds nanocomposite sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based and metal nanocomposite sensors are usually sensors composed of CNTs, graphene and its derivatives, QDs and various nanomaterials with metal compounds coordinated to modify electrodes. CNTs have excellent thermal, electronic, photon and mechanical properties, so that attracted great interests of sensing field [104,105].

Metal nanomaterials are widely used in the field of electronic sensing due to their organic combination of the chemical and physical properties of metals and the special properties of nanomaterials. Metal nanomaterials can be combined through hydrogen bonds, π-bonds, and antigen-antibody interactions, providing an excellent choice for the construction of sensors. Among them, noble metal nanomaterials also perform well in biosensing. Reports about carbon-based nanomaterials are increasing gradually, with unique structure, extremely high mechanical strength, good electrical conductivity, unique optical, electrical and mechanical properties, large specific surface area, good biocompatibility and other characteristics. Carbon-based nanomaterials have been widely magnetized in the field of modified electrodes due to their unique residual background current, large number of applicable potential windows, repeatability of sequential voltammetry scans, low cost, ease of fabrication, excellent stability, fast surface renewal, and compatibility with various safe handling of the modulator after use. Combining carbon-based nanomaterials and metal nanomaterials together to form nanocomposites with synergistic effects can expand the specific surface area of the working electrode and improve its performance. Electrodes based on nanocomposites are expected to increase the sensing capabilities of electrodes several times by reducing overpotentials and improving sensitivity. The performance of nanocomposite has been greatly improved on the basis of the original nanomaterials, which can make full use of strengths and avoid weaknesses. However, the synergistic coupling between these NPs remains to be explored, and what combination of nanocomposites to use for a certain class of drug compounds also needs to be discussed.

Metal NPs-functionalized graphene can increase the electrocatalytic performance, and so far, researches on the modification of graphene with noble metal nanoparticles such as Au, Pt, and Ag have attracted much attention. Ates’s group [106] developed a sensor based on AuNPs@graphene nanocomposites to detect vortioxetine (VOR) with a clear and irreversible oxidation peak, which showed excellent electroanalytical activity with a lower LOD as 50.0 nM, and the recovery result is satisfactory. Compared with traditional sensor, the addition of metal NPs such as Au and Pt in the graphene layer could produce significant electrocatalytic effects and enhanced the electrochemical reaction of VOR. Additionally, the modification of AuNPs on the surface of graphene has a higher electrocatalytic effect than PtNPs. Therefore, AuNPs modified sensor with graphene has outstanding electrocatalytic properties and is considered to be the most sensitive sensing tool for analysis of VOR. Carbon nanofibers (CNFs) are well-known nanomaterials for outstanding electrocatalytic properties. In terms of price, they are cheaper than CNTs and GR. Sakthivel et al. [107] modified the electrode with CNFs/Ru for CPZ detection with a LOD as 0.2 nM, which has been successfully applied with highly sensitive, selective and reproducible. Kubendhiran et al. [108] designed a novel electrochemical sensor based on active graphene sheets modified by RuS2 NPs, which was used to determine CPZ with a LOD of 8 nM. Shahrokhian et al. [109] prepared nanodiamond-graphite decorated with AgNPs (AgNPs-NDG) and used it to construct a novel sensitive sensor for determination of thioridazine (TZ). Compared with the bare pyrolytic graphite electrode, the oxidation peak current is significantly increased with a LOD of 0.01 µM. The prepared modified electrode has the advantages of simple preparation method, high stability and uniformity of composite membrane, high sensitivity, good long-term stability, and remarkable reproducibility of TZ response. The modified electrode can be successfully applied to the accurate determination of trace TZ in drugs and clinical preparations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General transition metal and carbon-based nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The modification of graphene with precious metal NPs has always been the focus of attention [110,111]. Transition metals have high performance-to-price ratio and wide availability. Ognjanovic et al. [112] designed TFP voltammetric sensor constructed by CNTs modified by Fe3O4 NPs with a LOD of 0.54 nM. GR and QDs have ideal properties in sensing applications. An electrochemical sensor was electrochemically modified with NiO/GQDs nanocomposites to determine CLZ, and the LOD was 0.55 nM [113]. Fe2O3 at room temperature has characteristics of pretty stability, biological tolerance and low toxicity. However, the conductivity of Fe2O3 is insufficient, so the method of decorating CQDs on the surface of Fe2O3 NPs is used to solve the problem of poor conductivity and sensitivity. Regrettably, CQDs with Fe2O3 NPs modified electrochemical sensors are extremely rare. Muthusankar et al. [114] synthesized sulfur-doped CQDs (S-CQDs) doped with Fe2O3 NPs (S-CQDs/Fe2O3 NPs) by hydrothermal synthesis. They explored the electrochemical sensing properties to detect OLZ. The sensor showed good electrocatalytic activity for OLZ ( Fig. 5).

El-Desoky et al. [115] synthesized Fe3O4-GR, and then modified Fe3O4-GR/CPE for the determination of OLZ. The LOD was 7.29 × 10−11 M, which had a good selectivity for OLZ in human plasma. Arvand et al. [116] considered the electrochemical behaviors of OLZ based on Fe3O4@Ag core/shell MNPs electrochemical sensor, which resulted a 5.4 times increase in the surface area, the LOD was 0.0018 mmol·L−1. The development of this graphene sensor modified by metal NPs can provide a tool for immediate medical applications of biomedical sensors. Sebastian et al. [117] prepared a FeNPs-loaded graphene oxide (GO-Fe)/3D ZnO nanohybrid sensor, which had a sensitive response to CPZ with a LOD as 0.02 μM ( Fig. 6). Punde et al. [42] modified CPE with CNFs/copper ferrite NPs composite (CNFs-CuFe2O4 NPs/CPE) to determine PMZ with a LOD of 2.3 × 10−10 M.

Aliasghar et al. [118] prepared bismuth NPs@porous silicon (Bi/PSi) nanostructures and constructed composite electrodes with CNTs for the measurement of TZ, and the LOD was 0.03 μmol L−1. The novel CPE of MWCNTs/Fe-co-doped titanate nanotubes (TNTs) nanocomposites promoted electron transfer and was manufactured for the electrochemical determination of SLP and the LOD was 8.7 × 10−8 mol L−1 [119]. Shetti et al. [120] proposed a carbon matrix sensor consisting of synthetic Ru-doped TiO2 (Ru-TiO2) NPs and MWCNTs to detect CLZ with a LOD of 0.057 nM. Kokulnathan et al. [121] prepared a stannous tungstate nanorods (β-SnW NRs) modified GCE sensor to detect CPZ, which has a fast and sensitive electrochemical response, wide linear response range, high sensitivity, low LOD as 0.003 μM and good selectivity for CPZ sensing.

Functionalized CNTs and QDs both have unique optical and electronic properties, so that they are often be used to fabricate sensors [122]. Compared with the use of QDs or CNTs alone, the combination of QDs and CNTs can perform better as sensing materials [123–125]. The carbon-based nanomaterials introduced before are often composed of nanocomposites in various combinations to achieve the effect of enhancing the detection sensitivity.

Behzad et al. [126] designed a MWCNTs with QDs composite modified sensor to determine OLZ. Under the optimized conditions, the LOD was 6 nM. ZnO NPs are considered as candidate materials worthy of applying in electrochemical sensing [127,128]. CNTs, GQDs and ZnO NPs are effective and reliable choices as electrode modifiers. Aftab et al. [103] designed a novel electrochemical sensor to determine the content of PMZ. The sensor is composed of ZnO NPs co-catalyzed by NH2 functionalized MWCNTs (NH2fMWCNTs) and GQDs with the LOD of 1.02 × 10−11 Mahmoud et al. [129] prepared a sensor modified by NCE and GQDs with nitrogen and sulfur (N, S@GQDs), which were used for electrochemical detection of OLZ with a LOD as 5 × 10−9 M, and had high sensitivity, selectivity and reliability to OLZ. Nigovic et al. [130] dispersed graphene nanosheets in a nafion matrix to modify GCE, compared with HPLC, the modified electrode is more convenient for the analysis of QTP in enteric-coated tablets, which only takes 40 s. Khan et al. [131] synthesized a nanocomposite based on MWCNTs/NCEs for modification of GCE to determine amitriptyline (AMT). Compared with bare GCE, the oxidation potential shifted negatively, indicating that the MWCNTs/NCEs-nanocomposite modified electrode had excellent catalytic activity for AMT oxidation with an LOD of 0.0845 μM. The method was effectively applied to the determination of AMT in drug and urine samples. This novel approach is presented here as an example of a fully developed approach for identifying AMT.

Electrochemical sensors are robust and sensitive, however, low selectivity is their main disadvantage. MIPs, as stable polymer nanomaterials with high selectivity to desired molecules, are widely used as recognition elements in various chemical sensing systems, especially electrochemical sensors. Carbon-based nanomaterials have attracted great interest due to their unique thermal, mechanical, and electrical properties. Combining the excellent electrochemical properties of carbon-based nanomaterials (graphene, CNTs, e.g.,), with the efficient selectivity and affinity of nanoscale MIPs to produce a new highly sensitive and selective electrical sensor holds great promise [132–135]. Due to the complexity of biological samples and the trace levels of drugs in biological samples, MIPs have been used to improve response signals, increase sensitivity, and reduce the LOD of sensors [136]. Motaharian et al. [137] developed a selective and sensitive sensor based on graphene-carbon paste electrode (GCPE) decorated with MIP NPs for the determination of chlordiazepoxide (CDP) in 2016. The adsorption capacity of the MIP NPs modified sensor for CDP is much higher than that of the sensor based on non-imprinted polymer NPs (NIP NPs). Furthermore, the use of graphene in the preparation of CPE can significantly improve the electrode response to CDP, with a LOD of 2.61 × 10−10 M. Then, they synthesized MIPs-MWCNTs nanocomposites for the determination of CPZ firstly. MIPs-MWCNTs nanocomposites were used to modify SPCE, with a calculated LOD of 2.96 × 10−10 M [138]. Hosseini et al. [139] used diazepam (DZP) as a template molecule to graft MIPs onto the surface of functionalized MWCNTs and used for CPE modification. The MWCNTs-MIPs modified sensors exhibited higher adsorption capacity than the MWCNTs-NIPs modified. Under optimized analytical conditions, the MWCNTs-MIPs modified sensors exhibited excellent sensitivity for DZP, with a LOD of 3.7 × 10−9 mol L−1. Alizadeh et al. [140] synthesized MIP NPs including highly selective recognition sites for fluoxetine (FLT) by precipitation polymerization, and combined with a CPE to construct a FLT-selective sensor. The response of the MIP NPs-CPE electrode to FLT was significantly higher than that of the NIPs modified electrode, indicating the excellent efficiency of MIP sites for target molecule recognition. It was found that adding a small amount of graphene to MIP NPs-CPE significantly improved the electrode sensitivity to FLT, and the LOD was calculated as 2.8 × 10−9 mol∙L−1.

Atta et al. [141] fabricated a sensor modified by FeNPs/ionic liquid crystal (ILC)/graphene complex for determination of TFP, and the LOD was 2.29 × 10−12 mol L−1. The sensor exhibits good stability, reproducibility, repeatability, anti-interference ability and selectivity, which will be used to determine TFP in real human samples and pharmaceutical formulations. Mohamed et al. [142] developed a composite CPE of β-Ni(OH)2 nanosheets, IL and graphene. The synergistic effect of the modifiers improves the electrocatalytic activity of SLP, and the sensor sensitivity is more than 2 times higher than that of unmodified CPE, with a LOD as 2.50 × 10−10 mol L−1 ( Table 1).

The rapid growth of the pharmaceutical industry is the result of rapid population growth, which inevitably leads to new biomedical and environmental problems. Drug overdoses are more severe than ever in many countries due to poor regulation. The National Center for Health Statistics reported a surge in antidepressant overdoses in 2017, resulting in more than 5000 deaths. Therefore, in addition to the quality control of drug production and drug delivery, there is an urgent need for accurate, sensitive and cost-effective technologies to track and monitor patient dosage [143]. Advanced analytical techniques such as GC-MS, LC-MS/MS, UPLC and HPLC are commonly used for pharmaceutical quantification. However, these techniques are limited due to their high cost and limited portability. Therefore, electrochemical techniques are attractive alternatives compared to the above-mentioned techniques [144,145].

In the past decade, nanomaterials have attracted much attention due to their excellent optical, electronic and catalytic properties. Nanomaterials possess high chemical reactivity and high surface area, providing stable immobilization platforms for drug molecules while maintaining their reactivity. Significant advantages of nanomaterials are their versatility and potential as non-invasive diagnostic tools, as well as their ability to combine multiple modalities in a single sample [13]. Consequently, considerable interest and significant progress have been made in the development of nanomaterials-based electrochemical sensors [146]. Combining nanomaterials with electrical sensors can improve sensor sensitivity by amplifying the electrode surface, increasing electron transfer, and catalyzing electrochemical reactions, and can also be designed to contain specific molecular recognition sites for specific target analytes. Electrochemical sensors based on nanomaterials have attracted great attention due to their advantages of high sensitivity and selectivity, real-time monitoring, and ease of use. Nanomaterials-based electrochemical sensors are of great interest for the detection and quantification of antipsychotics [134].

Significant progress has been made in the integration of different nanomaterials for modifying electrical sensors, with strong synergistic effects to facilitate catalytic reactions with target analytes. However, while this approach looks promising, it leads to other problems, such as nonspecific adsorption of dirt and other substances, which makes the proposed electrochemical sensors more difficult to apply commercially. Moreover, it is still unknown whether the addition of carbon-based nanomaterials such as graphene, GO, and CNTs will have a synergistic effect on the electrochemical detection of analytes. There is still a lack of intrinsic understanding of GO-based nanomaterials. For example, while sensors combining carbon-based nanomaterials with other modifiers such as metal NPs have shown good performance, it has not been conclusively verified whether carbon-based nanomaterials or other modified materials on their own are likely to produce comparable signals. Furthermore, the performance enhancement observed after modification with carbon-based nanomaterials may simply be caused by the increase in electrochemically active surface area. Additionally, while various promising electrochemical sensors have been proposed for a specific class of drug compounds, there are only some trends as to which nanomaterials may be more effective. For small-molecule drugs, electrodes decorated with NPs exhibit high performance, while for macromolecular drugs, carbon-based nanomaterials seem to be more effective, which depends on the interaction of the reactive site of the analyte with the sensor interface. Although most electrochemical sensors are tested with real samples, a considerable gap still exists between laboratory testing and the manufacture of sensing devices for commercial applications. In addition to sensitivity and selectivity, the cost and stability of electrochemical sensors must also be carefully evaluated. Therefore, the joint efforts of cross-industry and multidisciplinary research groups are crucial for electrochemical sensor development and commercialization.

In this review, the applications of various nanomaterials for the sensing of antipsychotics have been discussed. Nanomaterials can be used to enhance the electrochemical performance of sensors, so that different molecules and biomolecules can be identified. However, safety issues and expensive equipment used in the synthesis process hinder their use in commercial applications. It is necessary to develop safer and cheaper materials and methods to expand their applications.

The precision level of the developed sensor is not yet clear, and it needs to be evaluated by the LC-MS/MS method in the national reference laboratory. It is not fully understood why the results of electrochemical technology are different from the results of chromatography. In addition, sensors can provide consistency over time in monitoring the compliance and levels of interacting substances that are more important than the results of national reference laboratory measurements in the patient's body. Finally, through the further miniaturization and integration of these sensors, the treatment effect of schizophrenia can be improved.

From the published literature, the detection performance of the most modified nanomaterial sensors can only perform accurate quantitative analysis at the concentration of μM or above, and the LOD is at most as low as nM, very few can reach pM. Once faced with real samples such as human plasma, serum, cerebrospinal fluid, urine, feces, etc. With extremely low drug concentration, it is actually difficult to obtain accurate and stable test results without pre-processing due to their complex composition. Therefore, it is necessary to develop new electrode nanomaterials to enhance the comprehensive surface performance, so as to achieve ultra-trace analysis of drugs in a multi-component environment (especially without pretreatment), and greatly improve the practicability. In general, nanomaterials, as a kind of biomaterials, have great advantages and development prospects in drug sensing.
The authors declare no conflict of interest.
